stem: What produces partial saturation in a repeatedly excited tissue?

options.text: The echo is sampled after transverse magnetization fully refocuses
The phase gradient is applied after complete T1 recovery
The receiver accepts only frequencies outside the tissue
Incomplete longitudinal recovery before the next RF pulse

explanation: A short TR leaves incomplete longitudinal recovery, so repeated excitation reduces available magnetization. Tissues with different T1 values saturate to different degrees.

stem: How does a spatial saturation band suppress signal from inflowing tissue?

options.text: An RF pulse excites the band and a spoiler dephases its transverse magnetization before it enters the imaging region
A 180-degree pulse permanently removes spins from the bloodstream
A smaller field of view excludes all moving protons
The receiver bandwidth is centered on stationary tissue only

explanation: Spatially selective saturation prepares tissue outside the imaging volume so it arrives with reduced longitudinal magnetization. Spoiling prevents coherent signal from the prepared band.

stem: What makes CHESS suppression selective for fat?

options.text: A short inversion time nulls every tissue with a similar T1
A narrow RF pulse is centered on the fat resonance frequency
A gradient distinguishes fat solely by spatial position
A long TE removes water while preserving fat

explanation: Chemical-shift selective saturation exploits the frequency separation between fat and water. A fat-selective pulse followed by spoiling removes fat transverse signal while largely sparing water.

stem: Why can frequency-selective fat saturation fail near metal or at the edge of the magnet?

options.text: Fat loses its hydrogen nuclei near metal
T1 and T2 become identical in inhomogeneous fields
B0 inhomogeneity shifts local fat and water resonance frequencies
The phase matrix becomes independent of gradients

explanation: CHESS assumes predictable fat and water frequencies. Local off-resonance can move fat outside the saturation pulse or inadvertently suppress water.

objective: Compare CHESS with STIR after contrast.
stem: Which fat-suppression method is generally preferred when preserving gadolinium enhancement is important?

options.text: STIR with a fat-null inversion time
A saturation band placed over the lesion
No RF excitation followed by a long TE
Frequency-selective fat saturation such as CHESS

explanation: CHESS targets fat by resonance frequency and does not inherently suppress all short-T1 enhancing tissue. STIR is nonspecific T1 nulling and may reduce gadolinium-related lesion signal.
